Example: a field {"filled_pct": 0.42} becomes {"field": "filled_pct", "headerName": "Filled Pct", "cellDataType": "number", "formatterFn": "percent"}.. Publish it with manage_backends add.

tasks.title: Diagnose and extend Earnings Prep Data
tasks.prompt: The connected backend "Earnings Prep Data" shows only a validation badge in the snapshot, with a count of affected entries. Scan the served widgets against these desk conventions: Earnings conventions: symbol controls are endpoint params using /symbols; history charts use the standard 20-column chart width; single-value metric tiles use compact width 6. Fix every flagged entry, preserve healthy entries, and ship the queued addition in the same widgets_json refresh: `earnings_note`: name "Earnings Preview", description "Preview note for the earnings call.", endpoint /earnings-preview, type markdown, sized w=12 h=8 on the grid, params required: symbol — a dropdown (type endpoint), labeled "Symbol", options fetched from /symbols, default "AAPL".

tasks.title: Diagnose and extend Healthcare Research Data
tasks.prompt: Diagnose "Healthcare Research Data" from its served payload and count-only warning badge. Conventions: Healthcare conventions: Vega-Lite pipeline widgets use the chart-vegalite type; clinical readout date columns display as Readout; cached healthcare feeds cache for 15 minutes. Time-based config converts to milliseconds: "cache results for N minutes" means staleTime N*60000; "auto-refresh every N seconds" means refetchInterval N*1000. Refresh widgets_json with all widgets preserved or repaired, plus the queued widget `catalyst_metric`: name "Catalysts 30d", description "Catalysts in the next 30 days.", endpoint /catalyst-count, type metric, sized w=6 h=4 on the grid.

tasks.title: Diagnose and extend Rates Watch Data
tasks.prompt: The connected backend "Rates Watch Data" shows only a validation badge in the snapshot, with a count of affected entries. Scan the served widgets against these desk conventions: Rates conventions: Auction Calendar keeps Date as the date header; Rates Commentary series is an endpoint param from /series-options; single-value spread metric tiles use compact width 6. Fix every flagged entry, preserve healthy entries, and ship the queued addition in the same widgets_json refresh: `yield_curve`: name "Yield Curve", description "Plotly treasury yield curve snapshot.", endpoint /yield-curve, type chart, sized w=20 h=9 on the grid, raw true.

tasks.title: Diagnose and extend Vendor SLA Data
tasks.prompt: Use manage_backends refresh for "Vendor SLA Data". The snapshot's warning badge names no fields, so compare the served payload to the conventions: Vendor conventions: SLA status tables cache for 10 minutes; single-value breach metrics use compact width 6; incident notice streams are newsfeed widgets. Time-based config converts to milliseconds: "cache results for N minutes" means staleTime N*60000; "auto-refresh every N seconds" means refetchInterval N*1000. Then fix the bad entries, keep compliant widgets, and add `sla_runbook`: name "SLA Runbook", description "Runbook for SLA escalations.", endpoint /sla-runbook, type markdown, sized w=12 h=8 on the grid.

tasks.prompt: Four steps. One: add "Surveillance Data" at http://localhost:7807 serving `case_qa_omni`: name "Case Q&A", description "Ask questions over the surveillance case corpus.", endpoint /case-qa, type omni, sized w=20 h=9 on the grid, staleTime 900000, params required: prompt — a text input (type text), labeled "Prompt", hidden from the UI (show false), described "Question to run over the case corpus."; `alert_queue`: name "Alert Queue", description "Open surveillance alerts.", endpoint /alert-queue, type table, sized w=20 h=9 on the grid, staleTime 900000, params required: severity — a text input (type text), labeled "Severity", static options ["High"="high", "Medium"="medium", "Low"="low"], default "high", table columns required (field → header (type, extras)): alert_id → header "Alert" (text); desk → header "Desk" (text); severity → header "Severity" (text, titleCase render); age_days → header "Age (d)" (number, int formatter); `alert_metric`: name "Open Alerts", description "Open alert count.", endpoint /alert-count, type metric, sized w=5 h=4 on the grid, staleTime 900000. Desk convention: every widget this backend serves carries staleTime 900000, shipping app "Surveillance QA", description "Omni case questions and alerts.", tab `qa` named "Q&A" places: `case_qa_omni` at x=0 y=0 w=20 h=9, tab `alerts` named "Alerts" places: `alert_queue` at x=0 y=0 w=24 h=10. Two: instantiate "Surveillance QA" into "Surveillance QA Dashboard". Three: set prompt to "Show high severity cases" on the opened `case_qa_omni` widget. Four: note titled "Surveillance QA" with text "Surveillance QA is live from Surveillance Data: case QA ready."

tasks.prompt: End to end. Publish "Execution Desk Data" at http://localhost:7806 - widgets.json: `live_orders_grid`: name "Live Orders Grid", description "Streaming order blotter over websocket.", endpoint /live-orders, type live_grid, sized w=24 h=12 on the grid, staleTime 900000, wsEndpoint "live-orders-ws", params required: venue — a text input (type text), labeled "Venue", default "ARCA", described "Execution venue filter.", table columns required (field → header (type, extras)): order_id → header "Order" (text); px → header "Price" (number, showCellChange render) with renderFnParams {"colorValueKey": "px_change"}, data.wsRowIdColumn "order_id"; `exception_metric`: name "Exceptions", description "Open execution exceptions.", endpoint /exception-count, type metric, sized w=5 h=4 on the grid, staleTime 900000; `open_orders`: name "Open Orders", description "Live open orders blotter.", endpoint /open-orders, type table, sized w=20 h=9 on the grid, staleTime 900000, table columns required (field → header (type, extras)): order_id → header "Order" (text); symbol → header "Symbol" (text); qty → header "Qty" (number, int formatter); status → header "Status" (text, titleCase render). Desk convention: every widget this backend serves carries staleTime 900000. apps.json: app "Execution Live Grid", description "Streaming order grid and exceptions.", tab `orders` named "Orders" places: `live_orders_grid` at x=0 y=0 w=24 h=12, tab `signals` named "Signals" places: `exception_metric` at x=24 y=0 w=8 h=5. Then instantiate "Execution Live Grid" into a dashboard named "Execution Live Grid Dashboard", set venue to "EDGX" on the opened `live_orders_grid` widget, and leave a note titled "Execution Live Grid" that says exactly: "Execution Live Grid is live from Execution Desk Data: live stream ready."

Desk convention: every widget this backend serves carries staleTime 900000, shipping app "Vendor Notice Room", description "Vendor notices and breach count.", tab `notices` named "Notices" places: `sla_newsfeed` at x=0 y=0 w=20 h=12, tab `signals` named "Signals" places: `breach_metric` at x=0 y=0 w=8 h=5. Two: instantiate "Vendor Notice Room" into "Vendor Notice Room Live". Three: set vendor to "QuoteStream" on the opened `sla_newsfeed` widget. Four: a note titled "Vendor Notice Room" with the text: "Vendor Notice Room is live from Vendor SLA Data: notices ready."

tasks.title: Ship, open, and configure the Venue Packet Live app
tasks.prompt: Build, publish, open, configure, document. "Execution Desk Data" (http://localhost:7806) serves `venue_packet_pdf`: name "Venue Packet PDF", description "Monthly venue scorecard packet.", endpoint /venue-packet-pdf, type pdf, sized w=16 h=14 on the grid, category "Execution Reports", source "/reports/venue-scorecard.pdf", staleTime 900000, params required: venue — a dropdown (type endpoint), labeled "Venue", options fetched from /venue-options, default "ARCA"; `exception_metric`: name "Exceptions", description "Open execution exceptions.", endpoint /exception-count, type metric, sized w=5 h=4 on the grid, staleTime 900000; `open_orders`: name "Open Orders", description "Live open orders blotter.", endpoint /open-orders, type table, sized w=20 h=9 on the grid, staleTime 900000, table columns required (field → header (type, extras)): order_id → header "Order" (text); symbol → header "Symbol" (text); qty → header "Qty" (number, int formatter); status → header "Status" (text, titleCase render). Desk convention: every widget this backend serves carries staleTime 900000 and ships app "Venue Packet Live", description "Venue PDF, exceptions, and open orders.", tab `venue` named "Venue" places: `venue_packet_pdf` at x=0 y=0 w=20 h=12, tab `signals` named "Signals" places: `exception_metric` at x=0 y=0 w=8 h=5. Open the app as "Venue Packet Live Live" via manage_apps, set venue to "EDGX" on the opened `venue_packet_pdf` widget, then record a note "Venue Packet Live" saying: "Venue Packet Live is live from Execution Desk Data: venue packet ready."

rows.group: by tool family · 20 scenarios each · easiest to hardest

CALIB.label: GPT-5.5
CALIB.note: Frontier compression: composition (t4) barely slows it down; what remains are precision failures (exact layout coordinates) and ambient-state reasoning (the inspect family). This is the headroom warning, quantified.

CALIB.label: Claude Sonnet 5
CALIB.note: Run at temperature 0 via OpenRouter after a structured-output incompatibility was diagnosed and fixed in the harness (OpenRouter can route Anthropic models through Bedrock, where json_schema response_format degrades them to schema-minimal outputs).

CALIB.label: GLM-5.2
CALIB.note: Strong mid-frontier. Its t0 dip is a discipline trait — invalid tool calls and skipped prompt fetches on tasks it treats as beneath deliberation — not a ladder fault.

CALIB.label: gpt-4.1-mini
CALIB.note: The gating model. Strictly monotonic — this curve defines the tier bands. Its #1 issue is schema-before-create discipline, the exact behavior tool-surface fixes should target.
CALIB.role: gating model · tier bands defined here

CALIB.label: gpt-oss:20b
CALIB.note: Open-weights mid model. Independently reproduces the monotonic ladder — the cross-validation that the tier structure is real, not tuned to one vendor.

CALIB.label: Qwen3 8B
CALIB.note: The small-model floor: what an 8B can and cannot do against a real tool surface. Every failure here is headroom for distillation and fine-tuning.

SURFACE: Read the full workspace state: dashboards, tabs, widgets, layouts.
Switch the active dashboard or tab before acting.
Discover the widget catalog a backend exposes.
Fetch a widget's parameters before creating it — look before you touch.
Enumerate valid values for a widget parameter.
Read the actual rows behind a widget — the facts a note must cite.
Place a configured widget on the active tab.
Change an existing widget's data arguments in place.
Move or resize a widget to exact grid coordinates.
Remove a widget — and only the right one.
Write a durable note or generated chart onto the dashboard.
List and read the connected data backends.
List, read, and instantiate app templates into live dashboards.
Fetch a workspace skill: a reusable analyst workflow.
Read an MCP resource: the app-builder index, or a skill by URI. New in this release — previously zero scenarios touched the resource surface.
Fetch the server's own MCP prompts: tool-usage guidance and session context. New in this release.
Delegate workstreams to other agents with structured task requests.

swe.text: The agent receives a software issue and a repository. The benchmark checks whether the patch passes tests. The lesson for WorkspaceBench is the contract: same task, same verifier, comparable result.
swe.title: SWE-bench

terminal.text: The agent completes work through a terminal. The grader cares about final files, logs, and command results, not whether the answer sounded convincing.
terminal.title: Terminal-Bench

webarena.text: The agent operates websites through a browser. The lesson for WorkspaceBench is that UI and tool workflows need task-specific verification.
webarena.title: WebArena

workspace.text: The agent operates OpenBB Workspace through MCP-style tools. The benchmark checks durable workspace state: dashboards, tabs, widgets, layouts, and generated notes.
workspace.title: WorkspaceBench

command.code:
uv run --extra dev workspace-bench run-agent-command \
  --scenario gen_t1_create_price_performance_msft \
  --agent-command "python examples/openai_gpt4_1.py" \
  --run-dir runs/gen_t1_create_price_performance_msft \
  --json

# or compare models interactively, with repeats:
uv run --extra dev workspace-bench compare-models \
  --pack all --difficulty all \
  --repeats 3 --metric pass-at-k
command.text: The same harness runs oracle, noop, local model adapters, hosted model adapters, or any custom process that writes tool calls. Context arrives through environment variables like WORKSPACE_BENCH_TASK_JSON.

result.text: The result is not a vibe check. It is a structured scorecard: strict pass/fail, partial score (fraction of checks passed), and stable issue codes.

scenario.text: This is the real gen_t1_create_price_performance_msft scenario, abbreviated. Note the trace checks: the grader cares how the agent worked, not only what it produced.

toolcall.text: External agents do not need to import the benchmark. They write JSONL tool calls — one JSON object per line — that the harness executes and grades. This is the reference path for gen_t1_create_price_performance_msft.

issueMeaning.app_def_mismatch: an app definition field differs from the brief (tabs, groups, placement…)
issueMeaning.app_layout_overlap: two widgets in a built app tab occupy overlapping grid cells
issueMeaning.app_layout_ref_invalid: an app tab layout references a widget id the backend does not serve
issueMeaning.layout_mismatch: A widget landed at the wrong grid coordinates or size — the layout the prompt asked for was not produced exactly.
issueMeaning.layout_overlap: Two widgets overlap on the dashboard grid. A human opening this dashboard would see widgets stacked on top of each other.
issueMeaning.missing_app_def: a required app definition is absent from the served apps.json
issueMeaning.missing_custom_backend: the built backend never landed — manage_backends add/refresh missing or rejected
issueMeaning.missing_generated_widget: A required note or generated chart was absent, landed on the wrong tab, or its content lacked required facts (ticker, EPS, revenue…).
issueMeaning.missing_tab: A required tab is missing from the dashboard, checked by tab id.
issueMeaning.missing_tool_call: A required tool was never invoked — e.g. a delegation scenario where assign_tasks_to_agents never happened.
issueMeaning.missing_tool_result: The agent never retrieved required information through a tool — e.g. it wrote a note without actually reading the skill or widget data it was asked to use.
issueMeaning.missing_widget: A required widget was absent: wrong widget id, wrong backend origin, or wrong data arguments like the ticker symbol.
issueMeaning.missing_widget_def: a required widget definition is absent from the served widgets.json
issueMeaning.schema_not_called_before_create: The agent created or mutated a widget without first fetching its schema. Discipline check: look before you touch.
issueMeaning.too_many_invalid_calls: The agent exceeded the scenario's budget for malformed or rejected tool calls — wrong tool names, unresolved placeholder arguments, or ignoring ids returned by earlier observations.
issueMeaning.unlisted_widget_id: The agent used a widget id it never saw in a list_available_widgets result — in other words, it invented one.
issueMeaning.widget_def_mismatch: a widget definition field differs from the brief (endpoint, params, columns, grid…)

l0.text: The agent reads an existing dashboard and records what it found as a durable note. Sounds trivial, but it already tests whether the model grounds itself in actual workspace state instead of generic finance knowledge.
l0.title: L0: Inspect and answer

l1.text: Create, update, read, delete, or lay out one widget. This is where schema discovery discipline gets tested: list available widgets, fetch the schema, then create — in that order.
l1.title: L1: Single-widget operations

l2.text: Build a multi-widget dashboard from analyst requirements: right widgets, right tabs, right data arguments, valid layout with no overlaps. This is where composition pressure begins, and mid/small models start dropping sharply at t2+.
l2.title: L2: Dashboard construction

l3.text: Use app templates, parameter groups, workspace skills through get_skill_content, or task delegation through assign_tasks_to_agents. These test the richer MCP surface beyond widget CRUD.
l3.title: L3: Apps, skills, and delegation

l4.text: The workspace starts wrong: a stale ticker, a duplicate widget, overlapping layout, a missing tab. The agent must inspect current state, diagnose, and fix it without destroying what already works.
l4.title: L4: Repair

act.text: Then the actual work, through tool calls: create a widget, update its data args, add a tab, fix a layout, instantiate an app, write a note. One call per turn - the agent sees each tool result before choosing its next call. That's the Discover ⇄ Act loop in the diagram.
act.title: Act

discover.text: The agent is expected to look before it touches anything: list the available widgets, fetch a schema, check parameter options. I care about this step a lot, because it tells me whether the tool surface is discoverable on its own. Models that skip it end up inventing widget ids - and the grader has a check for exactly that.
discover.title: Discover

grade.text: The grader reads that final state first, then the trace: did the agent stay under the invalid-call budget, fetch the schema before creating, avoid invented widget ids, not spam snapshots. Out comes pass/fail, a score, and stable issue codes - so a failure tells me what broke, not just that it failed.
grade.title: Grade

scenario.text: One JSON file per task. It has the prompt the agent will see, the fixture backends to seed, the tools it may call, and the exact checks that count as success - required widgets, layout rules, trace checks. It also carries the labels I slice results by later: capability, workflow, domain, difficulty.
scenario.title: Scenario

seed.text: Before the agent takes its first turn, the harness registers the fixture backends the scenario asks for (four exist: Bench Equities, Bench Macro, Bench Portfolio, Bench Stark Enterprise) and seeds the starting dashboards. All of that data is fake and deterministic - no live market feeds - so every run of the same scenario starts from the exact same state.
seed.title: Seed

snapshot.text: When the agent stops, the benchmark reads the durable workspace state: dashboards, tabs, widgets, layouts, generated notes. This is what a person would inherit if they opened the workspace right after the run.
snapshot.title: Snapshot
